issue_title: Link_df runs very slow with large data sets
issue_body:
I'm attempting to link particle positions to form trajectories using link_df for a data set containing ~1,700 particles for 90,000 frames.  In attempting this, I noticed link_df was running very slow.  After 12 hours, the code had completed under 8,000 frames (I estimate that the entire data set would have taken maybe 4 days).  While I understand that that is a huge data set, I previously was able to complete the linking of the same number of particles (~1,700) for 24,000 frames, which took about 4 hours.  Naively, I was expecting the time to scale linearly, and so this 90k frame data set would take 12-16 hours (much more reasonable than 4 days).  But this was not the case.

My hypothesis is there is a memory issue.  The 90k frame data file is ~3 GB, and uses 10-15% of my machine's memory (linux server, found using "top" command).  Any suggestions on how this process can be sped up?

Some useful information, versions of various packages are: python 2.7.10, numba 0.12.2, numpy 1.8.2, trackpy 0.2.4rc7, and pandas 0.14.1.

Thanks for your help.

issue_title: Rotate Accepts in range [0,128) and returns in range [0, 1.0]
issue_body:
## Description
Rotate accepts input with RGB bytes and silently transforms it into floats in the range [0, 1].
I think either:
  Rotate and other transform methods that accept byte range values should warn when doing so, because the output format is changed.
Or
  Rotate and other transform methods that accept byte range values should return in the same format as the input.

If you guys can confirm which of these behaviors is preferred, or why the silent change is a feature and not a bug then I can either submit a pull request or go away.


```
from skimage.transform import rotate
import numpy as np

img = np.random.uniform(size=(10, 10)) * 128
img = img.astype('byte')
print(np.mean(img))
>> 68.8

img = rotate(img, 30)
print(np.mean(img))
>> 0.47
```

- [x] Python 3.5
- [x] skimage 0.13.0

issue_title: Idea: Concept of in_navigation expanded to specific navigation area.
issue_body:
For example one would be able to decide that the "custom quote" page is `in_navigation` but only shows in the footer navigation. The different navigation areas can be specified in `settings.py`, as those are typically statically defined by the website design, and then a staff member can select which navigation area(s) is appropriate for the specific page in the admin with a multi-select. The `render_menu` template tag would need a new argument to have a simple slug passed through to the `in_navigation` queryset method such as 'header', or 'footer', or 'mobile', etc.

Example:

    settings.py
    from django.utils.translation import ugettext_lazy as _
    ...
    FLUENT_PAGES_NAVIGATION_AREA = (('header', _('Header')),('footer', _('Footer')))
    ...

    template.html
    ...
    {% render_menu max_depth=1 template="fluent_pages/parts/menu.html" area="header" %}
    ...

issue_title: newChannel succeeds but netting channel not created
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

This is a report coming from gitter thanks to @ChosunOne using latest master. It is serious as it seems channel creation no longer works. It was noticed when the `connection` endpoint hanged for too long.

At its minimum the problem can be seen when the user attempts to create a new channel with someone (my node) and the [`newChannel()`](https://ropsten.etherscan.io/tx/0x1ff57f82855fd44f9ed252779c35c8b56bc1fed87114d91f2c432b751af5fa0e) transaction succeeds.

But in my end and in his end no blockchain event is seen and in his end there is this log entry:

`INFO:raiden.network.rpc.client new_netting_channel called peer1=000e6dfc netting_channel=00000000 peer2=9bed7fd1`

Notice the `00000000` for netting channel address.

## Tasklist

- [ ] Find out why this happens.
- [ ] Create a testcase for it and add it to our tests.
- [ ] Fix it.
- [ ] Add a changelog entry.

issue_title: Expose Raiden Rest API at raiden start
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

Currently raiden starts by exposing the raiden command line interface which is not what we need by default.

## Solution

Start the raiden rpc server by default instead of opening the command line interface and show an appropriate message to the user.

## Tasklist

- [x] Add two new arguments to the raiden executable. These would be `console` and `rpc` which would start raiden with the console interface and the rpc interface enabled respectively.
- [x] Remove the command line interface as the detault raiden interface.
- [x] Add an interface that will start the rest api server and provide a message to the user informing him how to connect to and interact with the rest server. Make this the default behaviour.

issue_title: Accept checksummed addresses for --address
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

When supplying an address in checksummed form (i.e. including uppercase characters) to the `--address` option a matching account in the keystore is not found. 

## Solution

Ensure `AccountManager` ignores case when handling addresses.

## Tasklist

- [ ] Convert all addresses to lower case in all methods of `AccountManager` that take addresses as parameters or read them from files

issue_title: Introduce network state for partner nodes
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

When Raiden is started we must not assume that all partner nodes are reachable

## Solution

All partner nodes must be set to the `unknown` state at startup and the health check should be kicked off at the node startup, once the Pong message is received the node state must be modified from `unknown` to `reachable` if a `max_unresponsive_time` timeout happens it must be modified to `unreachable`.

## Tasklist

- [ ] Add the state for the nodes in the ChannelGraph, the default value is `unknown`
- [ ] Once the first Pong message is received change the node state to `reachable`
- [ ] If a `max_unresponsive_time` timeout happened change the node state to `unreachable`
- [ ] Add a synchronization `wait_token_network` with this semantics:
  - Block until one of the nodes moves to the `reachable` state or all nodes are at the `unreachable`.

issue_title: define RESTful endpoint specs for API
issue_body:
Related to #10 

For filtering, should we only work with query strings on simple collection resources, or should we allow layering of resources :

for getting a specific channel:
- `GET /api/channels?partner=...&asset=...` or `GET /api/channels/<channel_address>`
- **vs.** `GET /api/partners/<partner_address>/assets/<asset_address>/channels`

for getting all channels with an specific asset:
- `GET /api/channels?asset=...`
- **vs.** `GET /api/assets/<asset_address>/channels`

for getting all channels:
- `GET /api/channels`

Should intersections of both approaches be allowed?:

 - `GET /api/partners?asset=.../<partner_address>/`

For putting data, should we stick to simple json-objects/form data attached to simple collection resource urls?:
e.g. `http PUT /api/channels partner=... asset=... settle_timeout=...` opens a channel with an additionally provided settle timeout.

One way to extend this even further would be to just use the encoded objects (e.g. channel)
to imply a client's request for state-update inside the node by changing the appropriate field to a new value.
The endpoint would always be `UPDATE /api/channels` and you could send an json-object
```
{
    'channel_address': '...'
    'partner_address': '...'
    'asset_address': '...'
    'balance': '...'
    'status': 'closed'
}
```
To request the closing of the channel.

### To Do:
- **please discuss:**

>  How RESTful does our API actually need to be, and how many duplicate endpoints do we need to provide for usability?

> How should posting to 'setting'  or 'state-updating' methods be handled?

issue_title: Reorganize conda to separate API from CLI
issue_body:
I would like to be able to do something which seems quite reasonable to me (psuedo-conda-api):

``` python
import conda

conda.api.create(name="test", pkgs='anaconda requests flask'.split())
stdout, stderr = conda.process.run(env='test', cmd='/path/to/cmd', args='a b c'.split())

conda.api.install(name='devel', pkgs='zip zap ping'.split(), dry_run=True) 
```

These seem like reasonable things for someone to want to do.  Certainly I want to do these things right now and have to figure out a way to do it, but, as best I can tell, this is impossible with the current conda implementation that mixes CLI into the API, such that there are operations like `sys.exit()` inside what should just be API calls (that should raise exceptions -- the CLI piece should catch these and then decide to report an error and do `sys.exit()`).

issue_title: Consider if auth tokens should be removed from channel URLs in conda-meta JSON files
issue_body:
Assuming conda is aware of URL-embedded auth tokens (see #5750 and https://github.com/Anaconda-Platform/anaconda-server/issues/1304) it seems like it might be problematic to embed them into the `conda-meta/package-version.json` files for a few reasons:

1. the package cache may be a shared resource and Bob shouldn't have access to Alice's auth tokens.

2. the auth token may expire or be denied and future operations that need to know "where to get the package" should provide a set of auth tokens relevant at that point in time (which may be the same ones), not assume that auth tokens used in the past are going to be OK.

It appears that `conda create --clone` uses this information (though for reasons stated in #5953 perhaps it shouldn't!)

Here are examples from files in my package cache:

`anaconda/conda-meta/history`
```
==> 2017-05-17 08:41:04 <==
# cmd: /Users/ijstokes/anaconda/bin/conda install --yes --json --force-pscheck --prefix /Users/ijstokes/anaconda --override-channels --channel https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/pro --channel https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/free --channel https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/r --channel https://conda.anaconda.org/t/ij-3e473a13-904f-433a-b6ad-SOMESTUFF/anaconda-fusion --channel https://conda.anaconda.org/t/ij-3e473a13-904f-433a-b6ad-SOMESTUFF/conda-forge keras tensorflow theano
+conda-forge::keras-2.0.2-py36_1
+conda-forge::protobuf-3.3.0-py36_0
+conda-forge::tensorflow-1.0.0-py36_0
```

issue_title: Bug in conda dependency solver
issue_body:
Attempting to install `trinity==2.4.0` results in `bowtie=2.2.5=0` being installed. This is not the lastest bowtie2 build (there is a build 2) and it doesn't work. Also, attempting to install `bowtie==2.2.5` results in the broken build 0 install. However, first installing `bowtie=2.2.5_py36_2` does work and subsequently installing `trinity==2.4.0` into that envrionment results in `bowtie2 2.3.3.1-py36pl5.22.0_0` being installed. Then everything works.

From a discussion on Gitter with @mbargull :
> Sounds like Conda's solver didn't prioritize its objectives (maximize req. pkg. version, maximize req. pkg. build number, minimize number of packages) correctly. The cause seems to be the dependency on pythonfor the newer builds which requires more packages to be installed. If you explicitly add python to the list of packages of your conda install/create command, you'll see it will then pick up the newer build. Interestingly conda 4.4.0rc1 behaves correctly.

issue_title: conda env create from environment file does not respect update_dependencies:False set in conda config
issue_body:
Even though `update_dependencies` is set to `False` (confirmed by running `conda config --show`) , when we create environment using `conda env create -f env.yml -n env_name` it still updates dependencies of packages listed in `env.yml`

For example, if I have `xyz==1.2` under `pip` in `env.yml` and `xyz` package has dependencies listed in its requirements.txt as `abc==3.2`, `conda env create` command tries to install `abc>=3.2`

issue_title: Conda confused about version ordering?
issue_body:
When trying to update my env conda states that it's downgrading numba:
```
The following packages will be DOWNGRADED:

    numba:    0.35.0.dev-np113py36_207    numba  --> 0.35.0rc1-np113py36_15      numba
```

Whereas my reading of [PEP440](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0440/#summary-of-permitted-suffixes-and-relative-ordering) would be that the LHS is 207 commits after the last release which came *before* `0.35.0` (i.e. `0.34.0.post207`) whereas the RHS is 15 commits after the release of `0.35.0rc1` and hence is a *newer* version.

This mattered because this made conda choose a bad install plan which I had to "fix" by pinning packages until numba was the package chosen to "downgrade"

issue_title: re-organize package cache
issue_body:
Further to comments from @mcg1969 regarding sym-linking, `urls` and `urls.txt`, and reliability of packges in the the package cache I have been inspired (again) to think that our package cache really should be creating a full mirror of repo.continuum.io, and in fact at even one level up in the case of multiple repositories.  I would like to propose that we re-organize the package cache to look like:

```
/path/to/anaconda/cache/reposite/platform/bitwidth/pkg-ver
```

I don't have a suggestion for how labels should be handled.

And in this way a simple `rsync` + `conda index` would allow someone (individual user, or IT administrator) to re-create a durable package cache that looks like a repository instance, and then conda could look there for symlinking or hard linking files and have a much lower chance of files being overwritten by conda in the `install` and `update` process.

issue_title: conda build test phase does not test package which was built
issue_body:
@jjhelmus commented on [Mon Jun 27 2016](https://github.com/conda/conda-build/issues/1051)

The test phase of conda build will pick up packages with the same build string in the main conda `pkgs` directory which can result in a test which should fail to pass.  An easy method to observer this is to create a recipe which passed, build it, then modify it so that it should fail in the test phase.  If the build number has not changed the working package  will be installed during the test phase rather than the newly built package.

A tell-tale sign of this behavior is that the package is not listed as being downloaded during the test phase.

I am seeing this behavior on a OS X system with the follow conda info:

```
$ conda info
Current conda install:

             platform : osx-64
        conda version : 4.1.3
    conda-env version : 2.5.1
  conda-build version : 1.21.2
       python version : 3.5.1.final.0
     requests version : 2.10.0
     root environment : /Users/jhelmus/anaconda  (writable)
  default environment : /Users/jhelmus/anaconda
     envs directories : /Users/jhelmus/anaconda/envs
        package cache : /Users/jhelmus/anaconda/pkgs
         channel URLs : https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/free/osx-64/
                        https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/free/noarch/
                        https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/pro/osx-64/
                        https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/pro/noarch/
          config file : /Users/jhelmus/.condarc
         offline mode : False
    is foreign system : False
```

---

@Korijn commented on [Mon Jun 27 2016](https://github.com/conda/conda-build/issues/1051#issuecomment-228721930)

`1.21.0` was the last version that did not have this bug. We've pinned the versions of conda, conda-build and conda-env across our whole system, many regressions are being introduced lately and it's... distracting, to say the least. :)

---

@msarahan commented on [Mon Jun 27 2016](https://github.com/conda/conda-build/issues/1051#issuecomment-228743126)

Thanks for the report.  This is a good excuse to finally have the test phase actually test the built module, rather than some strange series of "read metadata from built package, but then try to download package using that metadata."

@Korijn, my sincerest apologies for all the bugs I've introduced.  As part of #953, I have discovered that we only have about 45% test coverage (prior to that PR, most of our tests did not give us coverage information, because they run in subprocesses).  With that, breakage is somewhat inevitable. =(

I hope we'll have enough stability and new features to win you back soon.  Any tests you propose, whether as problem descriptions in issues like this one, as pseudocode, or as PR's, will be most helpful in assuring conda-build's stability going forward.

---

@Korijn commented on [Mon Jun 27 2016](https://github.com/conda/conda-build/issues/1051#issuecomment-228746997)

@msarahan Well, I'm married to conda already, so no need to win me over! I'm eagerly awaiting new versions to see if I can remove the version pins yet... :)

---

@jjhelmus commented on [Mon Jun 27 2016](https://github.com/conda/conda-build/issues/1051#issuecomment-228848290)

Minimal example which exhibits the broken behavior:

**meta.yaml**

``` yaml
package:
  name: foobar
  version: 1.0.0

build:
  number: 0
  script: echo "exit 0" > $PREFIX/bin/foobar; chmod +x $PREFIX/bin/foobar

test:
  commands:
    - foobar
```
- Run `conda build .` in a directory with this file, test phase should pass.
- Change "exit 0" to "exit 1", rerun `conda build .`, test phase should fail but will pass
- Change the build number to 1, rerun `conda build .`, test phase will now correctly fail.

Removing the foobar package from the package cache will also result in the correct behavior.

Some additional investigation leads me to believe that this is actually a bug in `conda` not `conda-build`.  Downgrading conda to version `4.0.9` removes this buggy behavior, upgrading to `4.1.0` and above causes the erroneous behavior.  Feel free to move this issue to the correct repository, ping me if you would like me to open an issue there.

---

@msarahan commented on [Mon Jun 27 2016](https://github.com/conda/conda-build/issues/1051#issuecomment-228849196)

Thanks @jjhelmus.  Considering that varying conda version changes behavior, I'm inclined to move this issue over there.  I will still be working to improve the way that packages are tested (more directly), but I think that's orthogonal to this issue.  @kalefranz @mcg1969, incoming!

issue_title: build environment shebang too long for Linux: "bad interpreter"
issue_body:
@janssen commented on [Wed Oct 12 2016](https://github.com/conda/conda-build/issues/1453)

I've got a build.sh which installs a tool, then calls it to install a couple of other things.  But the tool invocation fails:

```
/local/miniconda2/conda-bld/jkivy_1476297899236/work/conda_build.sh: /local/miniconda2/conda-bld/jkivy_1476297899236/_b_env_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_/bin/garden: /local/miniconda2/conda-bld/jkivy_1476297899236/_b_env_placehold_placehold_pl: bad interpreter: No such file or directory
Command failed: /bin/bash -x -e /local/miniconda2/conda-bld/jkivy_1476297899236/work/conda_build.sh
```

If you look at the top of bin/garden, you'll find this shebang:

```
#!/local/miniconda2/conda-bld/jkivy_1476297899236/_b_env_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_/bin/python
```

Now, that Python exists, and is executable.  And we know that the "bad interpreter" error refers to the shebang being too long.  It's about twice as long as the typical Linux max shebang length of 127.


---

@msarahan commented on [Thu Oct 13 2016](https://github.com/conda/conda-build/issues/1453#issuecomment-253517289)

conda takes care of this on package install by setting up a shortcut of sorts.  If you're installing that script yourself in build.sh, you'll need to do something similar.

https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/master/conda/install.py#L281-L289


---

@janssen commented on [Thu Oct 13 2016](https://github.com/conda/conda-build/issues/1453#issuecomment-253559603)

So the shebangs in the built packages are still bad?  Still seems like kind of a bug; shouldn't something like "#!/usr/bin/env python" be preferable?  I presume the whole chain of _placehold frobs are there to make enough space in DLLs to properly relocate the RPATH, but that line of reasoning doesn't really apply to shebangs.


---

@msarahan commented on [Thu Oct 13 2016](https://github.com/conda/conda-build/issues/1453#issuecomment-253564950)

Shebangs in built packages are bad until conda installs them and applies the function I linked above.

The placehold stuff is indeed there to make enough space in executables and shared libraries.

Are you proposing that conda-build apply some kind of shebang fixing before packaging?  If so, when exactly?  Conda-build cedes control to the user during bld.bat/build.sh execution, and then immediately bundles up files.  Is there a place where some intermediate shebang fix would help?


---

@janssen commented on [Tue Oct 18 2016](https://github.com/conda/conda-build/issues/1453#issuecomment-254662883)

Fair enough.


---

@jjhelmus commented on [Sat Dec 03 2016](https://github.com/conda/conda-build/issues/1453#issuecomment-264679973)

I've run into this issue while trying to rebuild some `conda-forge` packages with long prefixes.  Packages installed by conda-build into the build environment contain long shebang lines which cannot be executed in the environment.  Is there a work around for this?  Should conda-build be setting up shortcuts for these shebang lines?  

Specifically the example I've been debugging is building [`pkg-config`](https://github.com/conda-forge/pkg-config-feedstock).  This recipe installs the `autoconf` package in the build environment which includes the autom4te script.  This is a perl script with a shebang line in the environment of:

```
#! /home/jjhelmus/anaconda/conda-bld/pkg-config-feedstock_1480817967516/_b_env_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold/bin/perl -w
```

When this script is executed via the execution of `aclocal` in by the `build.sh` script it fails due to the long shebang line.

---

@msarahan commented on [Sat Dec 03 2016](https://github.com/conda/conda-build/issues/1453#issuecomment-264682025)

As long as the scripts being run are actually parts of some other package, this should not be happening - things should get fixed by conda when that other package is installed into the build environment.

I think what is going wrong for you, @jjhelmus, is that the shebang line contains an unusual space separator on the left.  I don't think it's syntactically wrong, but it might throw off conda's machinery for shortening the hashbang.

---

@jjhelmus commented on [Sat Dec 03 2016](https://github.com/conda/conda-build/issues/1453#issuecomment-264682952)

I worked out what is happening to shebang lines but I'm not sure if the fault issue is with `conda` or `conda-build`.  

The issue lies in shebang lines which contain a shace between the `!` and the path to the interpreter.  For example:  `#! /usr/bin/perl`. These type of shebang lines are only correctly updated to a "shortcut" prefix if the executable contains the text `python`.  

This is best shown with the following pair of recipes which creates three shebang scripts (foo) and print out the contents of these files in the build environment (bar).

## foo recipe:

meta.yaml
```
package:
  name: foo
  version: 1.0
```

build.sh
```
#!/bin/sh

echo "#! ${PREFIX}/bin/python" > say_hi.py
echo "print('Hello')" >> say_hi.py
cat say_hi.py
cp say_hi.py $PREFIX/bin/

echo "#! ${PREFIX}/bin/perl" > say_hi.pl
echo "print('Hello')" >> say_hi.pl
cat say_hi.pl
cp say_hi.pl $PREFIX/bin/

echo "#! ${PREFIX}/bin/lala" > say_hi.la
echo "print('Hello')" >> say_hi.la
cat say_hi.la
cp say_hi.la $PREFIX/bin/
```

## bar recipe

meta.yaml
```
package:
  name: bar
  version: 1.0

requirements:
  build:
    - foo
  run:
    - foo
```

build.sh

```
#!/bin/sh

ls $PREFIX/bin

cat ${PREFIX}/bin/say_hi.py
cat ${PREFIX}/bin/say_hi.pl
cat ${PREFIX}/bin/say_hi.la

exit 1
```

With conda `4.2.13` and conda-build `2.0.11` building the foo package adds three files to the ./bin/ directory.  Examining the contents of these files shows that there is no space in the shebang line of the file with `python` in the executable path, due to the logic in the `fix_prefix` function in the `post.py` module of conda-build.  The other scripts retain the space in the shebang line.

```
~/.../test/foo$ tar xf ~/anaconda/conda-bld/linux-64/foo-1.0-0.tar.bz2 bin/say_hi.py -O
#!/opt/anaconda1anaconda2anaconda3/bin/python
print('Hello')
~/.../test/foo$ tar xf ~/anaconda/conda-bld/linux-64/foo-1.0-0.tar.bz2 bin/say_hi.pl -O
#! /opt/anaconda1anaconda2anaconda3/bin/perl
print('Hello')
~/.../test/foo$ tar xf ~/anaconda/conda-bld/linux-64/foo-1.0-0.tar.bz2 bin/say_hi.la -O
#! /opt/anaconda1anaconda2anaconda3/bin/lala
print('Hello')
```

Now when these scripts are install into a conda environment with a long prefix as if done in conda build only the shebang line of the python script is rewritten as a shortcut `usr/bin/env` due to the SHEBANG_REGEX in the `install.py` module of `conda.

```
$ conda build .
BUILD START: bar-1.0-0
updating index in: /home/jjhelmus/anaconda/conda-bld/linux-64
updating index in: /home/jjhelmus/anaconda/conda-bld/noarch

The following packages will be downloaded:

    package                    |            build
    ---------------------------|-----------------
    foo-1.0                    |                0           2 KB  local

The following NEW packages will be INSTALLED:

    foo: 1.0-0 local

Package: bar-1.0-0
source tree in: /home/jjhelmus/anaconda/conda-bld/bar_1480823450512/work
+ source /home/jjhelmus/anaconda/bin/activate /home/jjhelmus/anaconda/conda-bld/bar_1480823450512/_b_eh
+ ls /home/jjhelmus/anaconda/conda-bld/bar_1480823450512/_b_env_placehold_placehold_placehold_placeholn
activate  conda  deactivate  say_hi.la	say_hi.pl  say_hi.py
+ cat /home/jjhelmus/anaconda/conda-bld/bar_1480823450512/_b_env_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehoy
#!/usr/bin/env python
print('Hello')
+ cat /home/jjhelmus/anaconda/conda-bld/bar_1480823450512/_b_env_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehol
#! /home/jjhelmus/anaconda/conda-bld/bar_1480823450512/_b_env_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_l
print('Hello')
+ cat /home/jjhelmus/anaconda/conda-bld/bar_1480823450512/_b_env_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehoa
#! /home/jjhelmus/anaconda/conda-bld/bar_1480823450512/_b_env_placehold_placehold_placehold_placehold_a
print('Hello')
+ exit 1
Command failed: /bin/bash -x -e /home/jjhelmus/anaconda/conda-bld/bar_1480823450512/work/conda_build.sh
~/.../test/bar$ 
```

I can see logic in having `conda-build` always rewrite shebang lines without a space and a placeholder or having `conda` rewrite shebang lines which contain a space.  Both could also be implemented. Personally I would prefer the later is that (with a space) is how I write my shebang lines and is how they were [first written by Dennis Ritchie](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shebang_(Unix)#History) 

Happy to open new issue(s) in either repository on this topic.

---

@jjhelmus commented on [Sat Dec 03 2016](https://github.com/conda/conda-build/issues/1453#issuecomment-264683083)

> I think what is going wrong for you, @jjhelmus, is that the shebang line contains an unusual space separator on the left. I don't think it's syntactically wrong, but it might throw off conda's machinery for shortening the hashbang.

Yes, that is what I found too, and these types of hashbang lines [are allowed](http://unix.stackexchange.com/questions/276751/is-space-allowed-between-and-bin-bash-in-shebang).  The question is should these be allowed in conda package or removed by `conda-build`.

---

@msarahan commented on [Sat Dec 03 2016](https://github.com/conda/conda-build/issues/1453#issuecomment-264683203)

I think all the code related to this is in Conda.  I'm transferring the issue over there.  Please ping me as necessary.

issue_title: conda env create --url https://raw.githubusercontent.com/.../environment.yml
issue_body:
I'm the maintainer for the datashader package, and I'm trying to streamline the process by which our users can set up an environment containing a bunch of libraries so that they can run our example scripts and notebooks.  These libraries aren't dependencies of the main library, and so they aren't installed with the main `datashader` conda install, but we do want people to be able to get them all easily.  For that reason, we provide an [environment file](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/bokeh/datashader/master/examples/environment.yml), but telling people to use that is complicated -- we either have to tell them to install datashader, then pull that out, or to go to the URL and download it using some platform-specific command or procedure, name it appropriately, then call it from the commandline.  We could put the environment on anaconda.org, but then it wouldn't track our changes in the github repo as we develop it.

It would be vastly simpler for us and for our users if they could simply do the same command on all platforms to get the very latest file reliably:

```
conda env create --url https://raw.githubusercontent.com/bokeh/datashader/master/examples/environment.yml
```
 
Would that be feasible?

issue_title: conda search should do partial name searches (regression)
issue_body:
The behavior of `conda search` used to be that `conda search X` would show all packages whose name contained the string `X`. There was a `-f` flag to search for full names.

Now it seems you have to explicitly add `*` to either side of the string to do a partial name search. 

I get that search now accepts a general package spec, but adding stars is annoying and unintuitive to beginner users. The most common desired behavior is to search for substrings. This is how the search functionality for every other package manager that I've used works (`pip search`, `apt-cache search`, `yum search`, `brew search`, `fink list`, `cabal list`, I'm sure you could find and try others)

I would suggest making search do a partial match search by default, as long as the search string doesn't include characters that would indicate it's a more general package spec. It can also use the `-f` flag, which was apparently kept but hidden from the help, to override this behavior.

issue_title: `process_xml` doesn't correctly parse `icon` in all cases
issue_body:
I've run into an issue where certain xml structure that contains `icon` and `cartridge_icon` does not parse correctly.  Take for example the xml from the recently added test case [here](https://github.com/pylti/lti/blob/master/tests/test_tool_config.py#L77).

```python
>>> CC_LTI_OPTIONAL_PARAMS_XML = b'''<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
... <cartridge_basiclti_link xmlns:blti="http://www.imsglobal.org/xsd/imsbasiclti_v1p0" xmlns:lticm="http://www.imsglobal.org/xsd/imslticm_v1p0" xmlns:lticp="http://www.imsglobal.org/xsd/imslticp_v1p0" xmlns:xsi="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema-instance" xmlns="http://www.imsglobal.org/xsd/imslticc_v1p0" xsi:schemaLocation="http://www.imsglobal.org/xsd/imslticc_v1p0 http://www.imsglobal.org/xsd/lti/ltiv1p0/imslticc_v1p0.xsd http://www.imsglobal.org/xsd/imsbasiclti_v1p0 http://www.imsglobal.org/xsd/lti/ltiv1p0/imsbasiclti_v1p0p1.xsd http://www.imsglobal.org/xsd/imslticm_v1p0 http://www.imsglobal.org/xsd/lti/ltiv1p0/imslticm_v1p0.xsd http://www.imsglobal.org/xsd/imslticp_v1p0 http://www.imsglobal.org/xsd/lti/ltiv1p0/imslticp_v1p0.xsd">
...   <blti:title>Test config</blti:title>
...   <blti:description/>
...   <blti:launch_url>http://www.example.com</blti:launch_url>
...   <blti:secure_launch_url>http://www.example.com</blti:secure_launch_url>
...   <blti:icon>http://wil.to/_/beardslap.gif</blti:icon>
...   <blti:vendor/>
...   <cartridge_icon identifierref="BLTI001_Icon"/>
... </cartridge_basiclti_link>
... '''
>>> from lti.tool_config import ToolConfig
>>> config = ToolConfig.create_from_xml(CC_LTI_OPTIONAL_PARAMS_XML)
>>> config.icon
>>> config.icon is None
True
>>> 
```

The issue appears to be the use of `in` used to check tag names of xml.  Presumably this is used to get around the fact that tag names include the namespace information.

```python
>>> icon_node.tag
'{http://www.imsglobal.org/xsd/imsbasiclti_v1p0}icon'
>>> 
```

This leads to issues when tag names are substrings of other tag names.  For example in the case of `icon` and `cartridge_icon` the config's icon is correctly set when it encounters the `icon` tag, but then overwritten when `parse_xml` encounters the `cartridge_icon` tag.

As this is potentially a larger change I thought I would raise an issue here first.  I would like to propose the use of lxml.etree.QName to check exact matches of the local tag name.  The existing tag name checks in `process_xml` would be changed to something like:

```python
from lxml.etree import QName
def _is_tag(node, name):
    return QName(node).localname == name

...

if _is_tag(child, 'icon'):
    self.icon = child.text

...
```

There is some evidence that this has been accounted for in the past using a serious of if/elif blocks. e.g.

```python
            if 'secure_launch_url' in child.tag:
                self.secure_launch_url = child.text
            elif 'launch_url' in child.tag:
                self.launch_url = child.text
```

That approach could be taken here as well, but it seems like this may warrant a more general solution.  I'm happy to provide a PR for this change if you would like to move forward.

issue_title: django-filter-0.15.0 breaks Graphene-0.10.2
Using Graphene + Relay + SQL Alchemy
issue_body:
I am trying to use graphene and respect relay specification. By database is backed uisng SQL Alchmey ORM.

```
class Product(SQLAlchemyObjectType):
    class Meta:
        model = ProductModel
        interfaces = (relay.Node, )

    @classmethod
    def get_node(cls, id, context, info):
        session =  context['session']
        p = session.query(ProductModel).filter(ProductModel.id == id).first()
        return p
        
        
class Query(graphene.ObjectType):
    product = graphene.Field(Product, id=graphene.String())
    node = relay.Node.Field()

    #resolve product
    def resolve_product(self, args, context, info):
        session =  context['session']
        id_ = from_global_id(args['id'])[1]
        p = session.query(ProductModel).filter(ProductModel.id == id_).first()
        return p
```

On resolve_product I always need to decode the global unique ID to a primary key database. Is it the correct approach? or Is it better to have another key on database called UUID for example?

issue_title: Filter with object using django-filter
How to create additional field in my schema that is coalescing two existing database fields?
issue_body:
I have `Building` SQLAlchemy table which is exposed with Graphene like this:

```
class Building(CountableSQLAlchemyObjectTypeRowId):
    class Meta:
        model = dbm.Building
        interfaces = (Node, )

    hers_score = BigInt()

    def resolve_hers_score(self, info):
        building = Building.get_query(info).filter_by(id=self.id).one()
        return building.hers_with_re if building.hers_with_re else building.hers_without_re
```

I want to add additional `hers_score` field that is a product of `coalesce(building.hers_with_re, building.hers_without_re)` fields. I hacked up the code above that works, but is terribly inefficient - it takes few seconds to resolve the list of buildings.

Due to my lack of knowledge, I'm wondering if this can this be optimized and done in a nicer fashion, and how?

issue_title: Missing stride argument in lasagne/layers/pool/FeaturePoolLayer
issue_body:
Commit a0961b1cf451255ea3372787d6ee4c78cee700a8 modified lasagne/layers/pool.py
method ```pool_output_length``` to include a stride argument, but ```FeaturePoolLayer``` makes a call to this method without this argument, raising an exception.

More details:

```class FeaturePoolLayer(Layer)```
defines the method
```
def get_output_shape_for(self, input_shape):
        output_shape = list(input_shape)  # make a mutable copy
        output_shape[self.axis] = pool_output_length(input_shape[self.axis],
                                                     self.ds)
        return tuple(output_shape)
```

which makes a call to pool_output_length, but is missing the stride argument
```def pool_output_length(input_length, ds, st, ignore_border=True, pad=0):```

Should stride be set to ds if None like with ```MaxPool2DLayer```?

issue_title: Add support for creating markdown programmatically
pyOpenSSL and service_identify should be in an extras_require
issue_body:
Since fido doesn't actually import anything from pyOpenSSL and is meant to be installed as a library, we shouldn't be depending on it as an install_requires.

Instead we should use an extras_requires (see [docs](http://setuptools.readthedocs.io/en/latest/setuptools.html#new-and-changed-setup-keywords))

This way we don't have to depend on cryptography which is a pain to compile.

issue_title: Broken interface definition for `unregisteredAdapter`
issue_body:
In https://bugs.launchpad.net/zope.component/+bug/158580, @zopyx reported:

> `zope/component/interfaces.py` contains this for `unregisterAdaper()` which is appearently wrong (seems to be the same docstring as for `registerAdapter()`)

```python
def unregisterAdapter(factory=None, required=None,
                          provided=None, name=u''):
        """Register an adapter factory

        A boolean is returned indicating whether the registry was
        changed. If the given component is None and there is no
        component registered, or if the given component is not
        None and is not registered, then the function returns
        False, otherwise it returns True.

        Parameters:

        factory
            This is the object used to compute the adapter. The
            factory can be None, in which case any factory
            registered to implement the given provided interface
            for the given required specifications with the given
            name is unregistered.

        required
            This is a sequence of specifications for objects to be
            adapted. If the factory is not None and the required
            arguments is omitted, then the value of the factory's
            __component_adapts__ attribute will be used. The
            __component_adapts__ attribute attribute is normally
            set in class definitions using adapts function, or for
            callables using the adapter decorator. If the factory
            is None or doesn't have a __component_adapts__ adapts
            attribute, then this argument is required.

        provided
            This is the interface provided by the adapter and
            implemented by the factory. If the factory is not
            None and implements a single interface, then this
            argument is optional and the factory-implemented
            interface will be used.

        name
            The adapter name.

        An Unregistered event is generated with an IAdapterRegistration.
```

issue_title: <dtml-in list_of_dicts mapping sort=name> breaks for duplicate names
issue_body:
Scenario:

```python
list_of_dicts = [{'name': 'a'}, {'name': 'b'}, {'name': 'a'}]
```

In DTML call:

```
<dtml-in list_of_dicts mapping sort=name>
```

It breaks because internally `dtml-in` changes the list to

```python
[('a', {'name': 'a'}), ('b', {'name': 'b'}), ('a', {'name': 'a'})]
```

There is no problem as long as the list does not contain duplicates of the sort key. If there are duplicates, Python has to compare the dicts when sorting. This works fine on Python 2 but it is no loner defined on Python 3.

I found out, that inside dvm.py especially the methods are read quite often but this list is generated for every call. Also in the Analysis class, the MethodAnalysis Objects would be generated on the fly.

For some random APK here are the results:

```
     5316    7.215    0.001   20.724    0.004 D:\test\androguard\androguard\core\bytecodes\dvm.py:7950(get_methods)

 10514785    5.203    0.000    5.203    0.000 {built-in method _struct.unpack}
  3041324    4.970    0.000    8.436    0.000 D:\test\androguard\androguard\core\bytecodes\dvm.py:3316(get_methods)
```

```
   162760    4.059    0.000    5.714    0.000 D:\test\androguard\androguard\core\analysis\analysis.py:55(get_instructions)
  5017750    1.200    0.000    2.861    0.000 D:\test\androguard\androguard\core\bytecodes\dvm.py:6568(get_instructions)
```

The execution time of Analysis is now slower, because all the MethodAnalysis Objects are created on startup. BNut the decompilation is at least 3 times faster.
In dvm.py:6568 there is also a cache implemented - maybe this one should be populated too while the first dissasembly? 5 million accesses to this method is much more than the number of methods...

I started to cache and precompile more things, but I'm not sure if this is the way to go. Maybe we should implement methods that do a precompilation explicitly, thus reducing the startup time but giving the user the possibility to do fast decompilation afterwards? Or should there be a flag/config option to not precompile certain lists?

Since `sniffer.py` and the associated functions were added to the certification tests, all tests have been broken on Windows. Extensive effort was put into refactoring `node.py` to allow for the tests to run on both Posix socket version of `otCli` and the `otNodeApi.dll` exposed on Windows.

A solution needs to be found to fix these issues. At the very least we should have the ability to disable the `sniffer.py` parts of the certification tests.

A better design would perhaps make a new interface in the `node` class for a node in 'sniffer' mode. It could then expose some functions for allowing the sniffer to get the byte buffers from that? For the Windows version, I could then implement the corresponding versions.

issue_title: Allow use of dataclass from python 3.7
issue_body:
Dataclasses are a new standard library feature to allow for less boilerplate in object oriented code coming in python 3.7. 

* [A short example](https://blog.jetbrains.com/pycharm/2018/04/python-37-introducing-data-class/)
* [PEP](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0557/)
* [current documentation](https://docs.python.org/3.7/library/dataclasses.html?highlight=dataclass#module-dataclasses)

For sacred, the only problem seems to be that since the `__init__` of a class is no longer explicitely defined, we cannot decorate it.
This is easily fixed though:

```
#! /usr/bin/env python

from dataclasses import dataclass
from sacred import Experiment

# this could easily be moved into the Ingredient class
def capture_init(ing):
  def decorator(cls):
    cls.__init__ = ing.capture(cls.__init__)
    return cls
  return decorator


ex = Experiment('dataclasstest')

@ex.config
def conf():
  factor = 2
  x = 2
  y = 3

@capture_init(ex)
@dataclass
class Vector2D:
  x: float
  y: float

  @ex.capture
  def scale(self, factor):
    print((self.x*factor, self.y*factor))

@ex.automain
def main():
  v = Vector2D()
  v.scale()
```

This results in the expected result:

```bash
(py37env) dwynen@taurus /t* $ python test.py -u
INFO - dataclasstest - Running command 'main'
INFO - dataclasstest - Started
(4, 6)
INFO - dataclasstest - Completed after 0:00:00
```

So *if* we want to support dataclasses (they are python>=3.7, so this is not a given) then it would be easy to add a decorator the the `Ingredient` class or to modify `@ing.capture`.

Thoughts? Opinions? :)

issue_title: Unable to chain `find_by_text`
issue_body:
I pip install'd from master so that I could get the newest `find_by_text` changes, which is great, however I find that I am unable to chain any `find_by_*` into `find_by_text`:

```python
import splinter
browser.visit('http://google.com/')
browser.find_by_css('div[id="viewport"]').find_by_text('Gmail')
```

Here we encounter the error:
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/Users/garrettedwards/auto_integration/tools/pythontest/testing.py", line 43, in <module>
    browser.find_by_css('div[id="viewport"]').find_by_text('Gmail')
  File "/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/lib/python2.7/site-packages/splinter/driver/webdriver/__init__.py", line 597, in find_by_text
    original_query=text)
TypeError: find_by_xpath() got an unexpected keyword argument 'original_find'
```

It just looks like there are some leftover arguments in the definition of `find_by_text`

```python
def find_by_text(self, text):
    selector = '//*[text()="%s"]' % text
    return self.find_by_xpath(selector, original_find='text',
                              original_query=text)
```

issue_title: Refreshing a live notebook with saved state
issue_body:
I'm not sure how big of an issue this is, but looking at the constructor of [WidgetManager](https://github.com/jupyter-widgets/ipywidgets/blob/a9c4069570aa92368f590296569e411d9c49e123/widgetsnbextension/src/manager.js#L55-L129), I notice that:
- If there is saved widget state, `set_state` is called, which checks for live widgets and connects to them etc, and creates any missing widgets. [here](https://github.com/jupyter-widgets/ipywidgets/blob/a9c4069570aa92368f590296569e411d9c49e123/widgetsnbextension/src/manager.js#L67)
- It checks if there are any live widgets on the kernel, and if so connects to them and creates the JS comms and widgets for them. [here](https://github.com/jupyter-widgets/ipywidgets/blob/a9c4069570aa92368f590296569e411d9c49e123/widgetsnbextension/src/manager.js#L79) and [here](https://github.com/jupyter-widgets/ipywidgets/blob/a9c4069570aa92368f590296569e411d9c49e123/widgetsnbextension/src/manager.js#L106)

This means that if you have a notebook with widgets, then save the state, and refresh the page without restarting the kernel, the JS comms and widgets are created twice. For certain widget configurations this can lead to an indeterminate state, as duplicates of widgets/comms are created with the same id (only the last one is registered with the manager). At least AFAICT.

issue_title: Test if multiple pulp_resource_manager can be run
issue_body:
https://pulp.plan.io/issues/898

Test if multiple pulp_resource_manager can be run without one interfering on the other. This will be a kind of redundancy test, if the original pulp_resource_manager get killed then the redundant one should take its place.

The proposed test path is:

* Run two or more pulp_resource_manager processes
* Check which process is the main (the current used one)
* Kill the main process
* Check if one of the redundant ones takes its place

This is a Pulp 2.10 feature.

issue_title: UnderstandingJwtBearerOptions RequireHttpsMetadata functionality
issue_body:
We would  like some help understanding the functionality set by the JwtBearerOptions RequireHttpsMetadata boolean option.

[The documentation](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/api/microsoft.aspnetcore.authentication.jwtbearer.jwtbeareroptions.requirehttpsmetadata?view=aspnetcore-2.1) for this option is somewhat sparse, and I have been unable to find a more complete description.

While the documentation states that this should only be set to false in a development environment, all of our services including the authentication (bearer token issuing) service operate exclusively behind a reverse proxy gateway, within which environment they are HTTP not HTTPS. 

The name of the option suggests that the token issuer needs to be an HTTPS endpoint; however, in our scenario this will not be the case.

Given the significance of proper security configuration I would like to know exactly what effect this flag has, how it works and what the implications of leaving it false in our scenario are.

We are not using OIDC or Identity, just the JWT bearer packages.

issue_title: build windows wheels
issue_body:
I have successfully build wheels for windows. For that I had to copy (manually or by a bat file) the 32- or 64 bits version of "libfftw3-3.dll" to the ".\build\lib.win-amd64-x.x" directory after the "py -x.x setup.py build_ext" command. After the "py -x.x setup.py bdist_wheel" command the wheel was created correctly.

If I did not copy the dll only the LightPipes.pyd file was in the wheel. I tried to use  "package_data" in setup.py  to put the dll into the wheel but that did not work. I also tried another directory structure with the setup.py outsite the sources directory, without success.

Copying was the only way I succeeded to get the libfftw3-3.dll in the root of site-packages along with the LightPipes.pyd file. This is required for successfully import LightPipes in Python. (Otherwise you get a DLL not found error).

Please let me know if there is another, more elegant, method to get the dll alongside the pyd in site-packages.

NB: I have updated the files in the LightPipes-Packages directory only (not in lightpipes directory).
Fred.

issue_title: Some `validatepath` methods return None
issue_body:

It may be a good idea to add a test to make sure filesystems do conform as well.

issue_title: Loading files as parameter fails using multiple config files with '<()'
issue_body:
We are trying to use the file lookup to load some external files for cfn-init/userdata and found a problem with our setup. A few weeks ago, a colleague raised this question regarding multiple configs: https://github.com/remind101/stacker/issues/403

The solution was just to use '<()' in the bash script to load multiple config files and pass a file descriptor to stacker.

It worked well until we started using file lookups in the YAML file as

If you look closely, /dev/fd/ is appended to whatever file I specify in the lookup configuration. We have debugged the stacker code and found that in stacker/util.py get_config_directory() stacker is not returning the actual config directory, instead it returns the file descriptor.

To sum up, the recommended way of loading multiple config files is not considering the way the file lookups are working. This is the first time it happens to us but I believe it will be a very common scenario from now on. 

I'd appreciate your input on this one.

issue_title: DSL for building stacks off other stacks
issue_body:
Would you guys be interested in a CLI script that would be capable of populating template parameters based on other templates? At my company, we've got about 30 CFN templates (and growing) for a single environment / DC. Needless to say, it can be a little bit of a mindless time suck to stand up a new environment or DC.

I've looked around at tools like [cumulus](https://github.com/cotdsa/cumulus) but don't enjoy that it's neither well-tested, nor plays well with partial "super stack" updates. I'm thinking it could be worthwhile to build something similar, but with an interface more in nature of Troposphere.

I was going to build this as a separate project, but since I'm already using Troposphere heavily, I figured maybe it could live under one roof.

Thoughts? If this is too much scope increase, I'd still be interested in your feedback on the idea itself.

issue_title: PuppetDB 4.2.0 Features
issue_body:
Update the API to provide functionality to new features listed in the [PuppetDB Changelog](https://docs.puppet.com/puppetdb/4.2/release_notes.html#section)
- New endpoint function, and possibly type, for the new Inventory Endpoint
- Add a `producer` field to the Catalog and Report types. This field represents the certname of the Puppet Master that sent the catalog/report to PuppetDB
- (Open for discussion) A `noop_pending` field to the Report type. This field represents a boolean flag indicating weather the report contains noop events

issue_title: Improve regression detection
issue_body:
Currently regressions are determined by comparing the previous build with the current build, and highlighting any tests that have passed but were failed in the previous build.

Sometimes a test does not run in a build for a variety of reasons, which leaves its status as "n/a". In this case, even though a test may have regressed, it will not be listed as a regression. An example can be seen [here](https://qa-reports.linaro.org/android-lkft/android-hikey-linaro-4.9-aosp/tests/vts-test/arm64-v8a.KernelProcFileApiTest/KernelProcFileApiTest.testProcZoneInfoTest?top=v4.9.95-639106-g25c5646b946c)

Other times, a bad baseline is used, which can hide errors. For example, let's say a release is tested (the desired baseline), and then a release candidate is pushed and tested, and then a very small change is made to the release candidate and it is pushed once more. Any failures introduced in the initial RC will not be shown in the subsequent RC, because it will use the previous build as a baseline, rather than the previous release.

To improve this situation, a test should be able to say "failing for N builds", so that it can be evaluated accordingly.

Lastly, some tests intermittently fail. This could be detected by looking through the individual test history of a failing test and determining if it is frequently failing and then passing again.

Then, failing tests could clearly show:
- New regression (i.e. this test was passing and it has now failed for the first time)
- Failing for N builds/Y days (i.e. this test has been failing for some period of time)
- Failing Intermittently (i.e. this test sometimes fails, and sometimes passes)

issue_title: Error refreshing token with custom RefreshToken model
issue_body:
Attempting to obtain a new access token using a refresh token results in the following error:

```
relation "oauth2_provider_refreshtoken" does not exist
LINE 1: DELETE FROM "oauth2_provider_refreshtoken" WHERE "oauth2_pro...
                    ^
```

I'm not sure what the problem is. My understanding is Django's "swappable" feature is supposed to use the custom model when defined, but that's not happening.

My code base has custom `Application`, `Access Token`, `Refresh Token`, and `Grant` models defined. We did this because we are not able to use Django to manage our database, so we needed to override the default table names. The implementation is at the bottom.

Access tokens are able to be granted and verified correctly. Digging in a bit, I see that the oAuth toolkit code has a reference to its own model (`oauth2_provider.models.RefreshToken`) at the time the above error is generated.

We are configuring the oAuth toolkit as follows (our custom models are located in a Python package called `fm-django-auth`):

```python
OAUTH2_PROVIDER = {
    'SCOPES': {
        'read': 'Read scope',
        'write': 'Write scope',
    },
    'APPLICATION_MODEL': 'fm_django_auth.Oauth2Application',
    'ACCESS_TOKEN_MODEL': 'fm_django_auth.Oauth2AccessToken',
    'REFRESH_TOKEN_MODEL': 'fm_django_auth.Oauth2RefreshToken',
    'GRANT_MODEL': 'fm_django_auth.Oauth2Grant',
}
```

Finally, our custom models:

```python
from django.db import models
from oauth2_provider.models import (
    AbstractApplication,
    AbstractAccessToken,
    AbstractRefreshToken,
    AbstractGrant
)

class Oauth2Application(AbstractApplication):
    
    class Meta:
        db_table = 'oauth2"."application'
        managed = False


class Oauth2AccessToken(AbstractAccessToken):

    class Meta:
        db_table = 'oauth2"."access_token'
        managed = False


class Oauth2RefreshToken(AbstractRefreshToken):
    access_token = models.ForeignKey(Oauth2AccessToken)
    application = models.ForeignKey(Oauth2Application)

    class Meta:
        db_table = 'oauth2"."refresh_token'
        managed = False


class Oauth2Grant(AbstractGrant):
    
    class Meta:
        db_table = 'oauth2"."grant'
        managed = False
```

issue_body:

### System information

- **Exact command to reproduce**:

<!--
You can obtain the Ray version with

python -c "import ray; print(ray.__version__)"
-->

<!-- Describe the problem clearly here. -->
## Comparing two different implementations of DDPG in ray/rllib:
* Impl 1 (open source implementation): https://github.com/ray-project/ray/tree/master/python/ray/rllib/ddpg
* Impl 2 (student implementation): https://github.com/ray-project/ray/tree/master/python/ray/rllib/ddpg2

## Differences in implementation and hyperparameters:      
https://docs.google.com/document/d/18yHw_J3y9SbtdCzLXz5miKm7UD1hZ57y1YEOd-VNN2U/edit?usp=sharing

## Differences in performance:
### Pendulum-v0

#### DDPG with smoothing
![pendulum_ddpg-ddpg2_100iter](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/7733195/41019356-1c27f7d6-6913-11e8-9911-db154494b3f7.png)

### MountainCarContinuous-v0

![mountaincarcontinuous_ddpg-ddpg2_50iter](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/7733195/41019392-3d427ef0-6913-11e8-9446-bbf43bac1e30.png)

issue_title: A suspected bug when use multi-GPU across nodes
issue_body:
I'm not sure if it's bug of my code or ray. 
The phenomenon is strange that when use multi-GPU across nodes, it just has a error of segmentation fault (core dumped). When the used GPUs do not come from different nodes, the code works ! (for example, set the number of actor to 4.  We have two nodes, and each node has four GPU)

Here is the error log. Yes, it is very short
```
**_Segmentation fault (core dumped)_**
```

Here is my code (I have removed the irrelevant part)
Firstly, let us define an actor.
```
import ray
ray.init(redis_address="9.91.9.123:6379")
class test(object):
    def __init__(self, model_config):
        import os

        os.environ['CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES'] = ','.join([str(i) for i in ray.get_gpu_ids()])
        from keras.models import model_from_config
        self.model = model_from_config(model_config, custom_objects={})
        self.weights = None
    def run(self):
        for i in range(1000):
            self.model.predict(np.zeros((100,1,4)))
        return 1

    def get_weights(self):
        import numpy as np
        return [np.zeros((2,2)),np.zeros((2,2))]

    def set_weights(self, weights):
        self.weights = weights
```
Then i define the model
```
import numpy as np
import gym
import ray
from keras.models import Sequential
from keras.layers import Dense, Activation, Flatten

model = Sequential()
model.add(Flatten(input_shape=(1,) + (4,)))
model.add(Dense(16))
model.add(Activation('relu'))
model.add(Dense(16))
model.add(Activation('relu'))
model.add(Dense(16))
model.add(Activation('relu'))
model.add(Dense(2))
model.add(Activation('linear'))
```
then i start to train on two nodes, and each node has four GPUs.
The following code looks a bit complicated at first glance. In fact, 
it is a simple averaging operation between multiple agents' return weights
```
config = {
    'class_name': model.__class__.__name__,
    'config': model.get_config(),
}

agent_count = 8

cpu_actor = ray.actor(num_gpus=1)(test)
agents =[cpu_actor(config) for _ in range(agent_count)]
rollout = 20
for k in range(rollout):
    rewards=[]
    actions = []
    weights=[]
    for i in range(agent_count):
        actions.append(agents[i].run())
    for i in range(agent_count):
        id,actions = ray.wait(actions)
        rewards.append(ray.get(id))
    for i in range(agent_count):
        weights.append(ray.get(agents[i].get_weights()))
    newweights=[]
    for i in range(len(weights[0])):
        temp = np.zeros(weights[0][i].shape)
        for j in range(agent_count):
            temp += weights[j][i]/agent_count
        newweights.append(temp)
    idcol = []
    for i in range(agent_count):
        idcol.append(agents[i].set_weights(newweights))
    for i in range(agent_count):
        ray.wait(idcol)
    print("step {},reward {}".format(k,np.mean(rewards)))
```

issue_title: Cannot register custom serializers before ray.init().
issue_body:
```python
import ray

class Foo:
    pass

def s(obj):
    return obj

def d(obj):
    return obj

ray.register_custom_serializer(Foo, serializer=s, deserializer=d)
```

Fails with

```
AttributeError                            Traceback (most recent call last)
<ipython-input-5-ab10f55b546d> in <module>()
----> 1 ray.register_custom_serializer(Foo, serializer=s, deserializer=d)

~/Workspace/ray/python/ray/worker.py in register_custom_serializer(cls, use_pickle, use_dict, serializer, deserializer, local, driver_id, class_id, worker)
   2421 
   2422     if driver_id is None:
-> 2423         driver_id_bytes = worker.task_driver_id.id()
   2424     else:
   2425         driver_id_bytes = driver_id.id()

AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'id'
```

Probably introduced in https://github.com/ray-project/ray/pull/2406.

It should be possible to define custom serializers before `ray.init()` (e.g., in other Python modules). The solution is probably to allow the driver ID to be `None` when `register_custom_serializer` is cached and then pass in the driver ID when it actually gets run when `ray.init()` is called.

issue_title: containers.list() fails if a container is removed while it's being called
issue_body:
I'm getting the following error in an application that uses docker-py with fairly heavy container churn. 

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "executor.py", line 44, in event_loop
    num_executors = len(get_executor_containers())
  File "executor.py", line 186, in get_executor_containers

  File "/home/ben/envs/executor/lib/python3.6/site-packages/docker/models/containers.py", line 767, in list

  File "/home/ben/envs/executor/lib/python3.6/site-packages/docker/models/containers.py", line 767, in <listcomp>
    return [self.get(r['Id']) for r in resp]
  File "/home/ben/envs/executor/lib/python3.6/site-packages/docker/models/containers.py", line 720, in get
    resp = self.client.api.inspect_container(container_id)
  File "/home/ben/envs/executor/lib/python3.6/site-packages/docker/utils/decorators.py", line 21, in wrapped
    return f(self, resource_id, *args, **kwargs)
  File "/home/ben/envs/executor/lib/python3.6/site-packages/docker/api/container.py", line 745, in inspect_container
    self._get(self._url("/containers/{0}/json", container)), True
  File "/home/ben/envs/executor/lib/python3.6/site-packages/docker/api/client.py", line 220, in _result
    self._raise_for_status(response)
  File "/home/ben/envs/executor/lib/python3.6/site-packages/docker/api/client.py", line 216, in _raise_for_status
    raise create_api_error_from_http_exception(e)
  File "/home/ben/envs/executor/lib/python3.6/site-packages/docker/errors.py", line 30, in create_api_error_from_http_exception
    raise cls(e, response=response, explanation=explanation)
docker.errors.NotFound: 404 Client Error: Not Found for url: http+docker://localunixsocket/v1.24/containers/762ad9c915018c1d38c0ae71ab44ce69b3750b70b0524d0ec6e01dd62bd7fe48/json ("No such container: 762ad9c915018c1d38c0ae71ab44ce69b3750b70b0524d0ec6e01dd62bd7fe48")
```

It seems to me that list() gets all containers from the api, then *while* it's iterating through that list, a container in the list is removed. This causes the call to list() to fail with a NotFound exception.

I think it could be fixed by replacing models/containers.py:767
```python
return [self.get(r['Id']) for r in resp]
```
with
```python
containers = []
for r in resp:
    try:
        containers.append(self.get(r['id']))
    except NotFound: # a container may have been removed while iterating
        pass
return containers
```
but I'm not sure if that would cause unforeseen issues.

I'm seeing this with a few (3 or 4) containers running at a time, but I think the issue would be much worse if you had many containers running at once.

issue_title: Flawed sync --keepDays semantics
issue_body:
Currently `b2 sync --keepDays 30` deletes file versions older than 30 days, by looking at the modification time of the file. This is fatally flawed. If I have a file that was last modified a year ago, and I accidentally delete it, and sync, the sync will delete the file (because version.modtime = 1 year, > 30 days), leaving only a "hidden" marker. Even though I asked to keep 30 days of history, I can't undo a mistake I did a minute ago and my data is gone.

I think it should use the upload timestamp instead.

issue_title: syncing progress speed display
issue_body:
I noticed a small thing that may be somewhat misleading. When using b2 sync ... the progress tracker speed seems to be an average from the whole syncing progress, or quite a long period at least, rather than the current speed.

Bug or working as intended? I for one would have expected it to be the average for the last few seconds or so, it seems to me that this is more common (and expected) in progress displays.

issue_title: Test invalid curve points
issue_body:
Add test cases to verify that we handle invalid curve points correctly. @simo5 was brave enough to jump head-first into OpenSSL. It looks like we are safe. Let's add some tests to be sure.

```
in cryptography load_elliptic_curve_public_numbers() calls _ec_key_set_public_key_affine_coordinates()
in that function EC_KEY_set_public_key_affine_coordinates() is called from openssl
and inside openssl the last function called in the method is: EC_KEY_check_key()
jose calls EC_KEY_check_key() too
```

issue_title: update pinv calls
issue_body:
The `pinv` calls use low level lapack wrappers.  There are several issues with this:

1. difficulty to maintain #216, #209, #213 
2. speed -- we make a claim that it's faster (see below)
3. code bloat

This https://gist.github.com/lukeolson/529c010c475fdaca9e741ddc2dba3b43

![test](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1615863/35699170-e4031694-0754-11e8-91a8-f0677d541ab3.png)


Suggests simply using `numpy.linalg.pinv`

@jbschroder what do you think

export CPLUS_INCLUDE_PATH=$CPLUS_INCLUDE_PATH:/path/to/dynet:/path/to/boost-1.60/include/boost-1_60:/path/to/eigen

issue_title: Large files in repository history
issue_body:
There are a few large files in the repository history (at some point someone checked in `dynet.so` and `_dynet.so`) that cause cloning to take forever on a slow internet connect.

I'll push a commit shortly to fix this.

issue_title: Segfault on CUDA when using GCC 4.9.3 and optimization -O2
issue_body:
Currently, when using cnn on CUDA, compiling with GCC 4.9.3, segfaults occur when using the default optimization setting of -O2. This is not a problem on GCC 4.8.*, but may be a problem on other versions of GCC.

We don't currently know the cause, but a patch fixing the bug would be welcome. If you want a quick fix, find the line including `APPEND CUDA_NVCC_FLAGS` in `cnn/CMakeLists.txt` and change `-O2` to `-O1`.

issue_title: Rebuild only selected packages
issue_body:
Hi guys, I don't seem to be able to rebuild a single package at a time when I'm using buildozer and python-for-android.  I don't know if this is a buildozer or a python-for-android problem - but thought I'd start here.

Some of my app involves compiled cython code that is android specific (i.e. I can't test it on Linux/Windows).  I have put this portion of my app in a recipe for P4A which I can compile and package up using buildozer

However, whenever I make a change to my cython package the only way I seem to be able to get the changes into my android app so that I can test them is to rebuild my entire app from scratch using buildozer - including recompiling python and kivy which take a long time to recompile.

I've tried using the "update" option with buildozer, which claims to only update the given packages, but this appears to just start the build process again from scratch (python and kivy are recompiled).

Am I missing something or doing something wrong?

Many thanks!  Loving being able to code apps in Python - Kivy is an awesome project...

issue_title: [moved] pyo files are not being recreated by ./build.py in python for android
issue_body:
moved from [kivy/kivy](https://github.com/kivy/kivy/issues/1961)

originally reported by @blissGitHub:

    I wrote the app on desktop and tested it.
    It worked fine.
    So I then compiled it through the ubuntu vm provided by kivy.
    Initially the pyo files were created.  After I realised that the code changes I've been making is not     
    being applied in p4a, I decided to delete the pyo files.
    After that I did the ./distribute.sh -m "kivy" and then did the ./build.py....
    this time however the .pyo files were not recreated and eventually I found out that my apk crashes.
    So I had to manually create it through:
    "python -O -m compileall /path/to/files"

    Then ran ./build.py  again....this time it worked......

issue_title: standalone="no" breaks some images
issue_body:
I'm using Scour to optimize UI elements in Firefox. On some files this appears to break.

Scour adds **standalone="no"** to the header. This makes the file unable to be opened in Viewnior. If used as a Firefox UI element, it also fails to render (curiously it works if opened by Firefox as a page)

I don't understand what standalone means in the context of SVGs, so I can't say if this is a valid bug.

The file in question:
[menu-check-black.svg.zip](https://github.com/codedread/scour/files/201363/menu-check-black.svg.zip)

issue_title: standardize (and create guidelines for) pykechain architecture
issue_body:
Since pykechain is expanding rapidly, we might want to establish some guidelines regarding the architecture. This would also involve refactoring the current code to follow these guidelines.

Remarks regarding the codebase:
- The `Client` has a private `_request` method and is responsible for building requests (e.g. `Client.activites` and `Client.create_part`), however sometimes this is violated; e.g. `Property.delete` and `Activity.configure` build a request themselves.
- Most of the `APIError`s do not include additional information (e.g. why could a new activity not be created?)

issue_title: need a different way to handle polyfills
issue_body:
Currently we use polyfills.io webservice but this won't work in restricted environments rights @BerryDaniel ?

cc @milafrerichs

issue_title: User defined timesteps
issue_body:
Allow the timestep to be user defined rather than being fixed at one year. 

Will require some thinking about how we do this e.g. do we let the user supply yearly emissions and then have FAIR linearly interpolate if they want to use smaller (or bigger) timesteps or do we make it that the emissions inputs define the timesteps with some flag to indicate whether the timeseries is days, months, years etc.

Trying to create a plan with a value for 'interval' that is invalid (in this case 'daily') raises an exception that is inaccurate:

Only 'month', 'week' and 'year'  are listed, but the attributed also accepts 'day'.

issue_title: Problems with Stripe version v1.28.0
issue_body:
I am using **stripe v1.28.0**. Creating a simple script ``stripe_test.py`` with the following code:

    import stripe

    stripe.api_key = 'sk_test_XYZ'
    stripe.api_version = '2015-10-16'
    stripe.Balance.retrieve()

If I execute the script ``$ python stripe_test.py`` I got  the following error:
    
    line 5, in <module>
    stripe.Balance.retrieve()
    AttributeError: 'module' object has no attribute 'Balance'

Nonetheless if I run this code on python console it works fine. Any idea of what's going on?

issue_title: memory allocation based on assumed number of items as function of max(id)
issue_body:
Hi,

from the docs:
"""
Right now it only accepts integers as identifiers for items. Note that it will allocate memory for max(id)+1 items because it assumes your items are numbered 0 … n-1. If you need other id's, you will have to keep track of a map yourself.
"""

It still would be nice if there would be option to provide the max num of items to be indexed as parameter, rather than just using max(id) +1.

issue_title: segfault 11 when using non sequential IDs
issue_body:
I just came upon a reproducible segfault that seems to be related to non-sequential IDs.

I am working with 10-dimensional vectors and for this small reproducible example, 200 items. When inserting non sequential IDs, if I attempt to `get_nns_by_vector` with a `n` value of 100, I get a segfault. Calling it with a `n` of 10 does work. The specific vector that's passed to `get_nns_by_vector` also makes a difference (see comments in code below)

Please get the values [from this gist](https://gist.github.com/gregsadetsky/c32315dd7825a40d11b2dccc09f40468).

```python
from annoy import AnnoyIndex
import numpy as np

# path to values.txt file
VALUES_PATH = 'annoy_segfault_values.txt'
# path to temporary annoy index file
ANNOY_INDEX_PATH = 'test.ann'

NMB_VECTORS = 10
NMB_TREES = 20

t = AnnoyIndex(NMB_VECTORS, metric='euclidean')
with open(VALUES_PATH) as f:
  for line_idx, line in enumerate(f):
    idx, vector = line.strip().split(';')
    idx = int(idx)
    vector = np.fromstring(vector, dtype='float64', sep=' ')
    # using 'line_idx' in the line below instead of 'idx'
    # (i.e., using sequential IDs) fixes the issue
    t.add_item(idx, vector)

t.build(NMB_TREES)
t.save(ANNOY_INDEX_PATH)

# .....

u = AnnoyIndex(NMB_VECTORS, metric='euclidean')
u.load(ANNOY_INDEX_PATH)

# segfault only happens with certain vectors.
# for instance, np.zeros(10) does not segfault.
# the term at index 4 must be approximately >=0.9
# to reproduce the segfault
needle_vector = np.array([0., 0., 0., 0., 0.9, 0., 0., 0., 0., 0.])
needle_vector = needle_vector.astype('float64')

# segfault 11
out = u.get_nns_by_vector(needle_vector, 100)
print out
```

---

As a semi-related, but different issue, if I switch the metric to `manhattan` (which I should probably use in this case), one of the returned result indices is `0`, which was not part of the original indices.

switch
```python
t = AnnoyIndex(NMB_VECTORS, metric='manhattan')
```
and
```python
u = AnnoyIndex(NMB_VECTORS, metric='manhattan')
```
in the code above to see the issue

issue_title: Long tuples in map reduce
issue_body:
Hello!

I have started to use luigi for hadoop streaming. Jobs with "key\tvalue" records work fine. But when I started to use "key\tvalue1\tvalue2..." tuples, reduce function receives value1 only. Reason is in ```hadoop.JobTask._reduce_input``` function:

```
def _reduce_input(self, inputs, reducer, final=NotImplemented):
        """
        Iterate over input, collect values with the same key, and call the reducer for each unique key.
        """
        for key, values in groupby(inputs, key=lambda x: self.internal_serialize(x[0])):
            for output in reducer(self.deserialize(key), (v[1] for v in values)):
                yield output
        if final != NotImplemented:
            for output in final():
                yield output
        self._flush_batch_incr_counter()
```

Should it be ```v[1:]``` instead of ```v[1]```?
Is it bug or feature?

I can always redefine this function in subclass, but it looks like a bug.

issue_title: Feature request: pandoc-style implicit link definition for headings
issue_body: Hotdoc can currently do this: ```markdown ## Some heading blah blah ## Some other heading As I said in [](#some-heading), ... ``` and have it interpreted as "As I said in _Some other heading_, ..." with a hyperlink. This works because it automatically generates `` from the text of the heading itself, and because as a (Hotdoc-specific?) shortcut, omitting the title of a hyperlink makes Hotdoc infer it from the destination. This requires the author of the Markdown document to know how Hotdoc will mangle a heading title into a `string-suitable-for-anchors`, which is not necessarily trivial if it contains punctuation or even markup. Pandoc's spelling for this (which seems to be a commonly available shortcut in other parsers) is: ```markdown ## Some heading blah blah ## Some other heading As I said in [Some heading], ... ``` (or equivalently the same thing with `[Some heading][]`). Effectively, it behaves as though the author had written the first section as: ```markdown ## Some heading blah blah [Some heading]: #some-heading ``` except that if the author writes an explicit link definition `[Some heading]: ...`, it takes precedence over the one generated by this shortcut. This seems very much like the [Markdown philosophy](https://daringfireball.net/projects/markdown/syntax#philosophy): the writer writes nice human-readable text that matches the heading, and Pandoc transforms it into the machine-readable form (the anchor syntax).

After analyzing the problem, it is caused when normalizing quaternion, which call the "/=" operator that I changed above. The "/=" op has a precession error in this situation, which yields to epsilon quaternion, which is shown in the back trace.

I've seen this issue while on my Bullet3 C API fork (while merging original branch).

It is weird that btAssert() is saving the precession.
This might even be a compiler bug, but I'm not sure.
It might be the cause for #180

Please try my change test and tell me if you have crush in debug64 mode.

issue_title: Refactor settings with defaults
issue_body:
* [ ] Centralize default values for all settings
* [ ] Provide way to easy add custom default values
* [ ] Override defaults with environment variables ``CLIQUET_STORAGE_URL``

For example : 

```python
from cliquet import initialize_cliquet, DEFAULT_CONFIG

app_defaults = DEFAULT_CONFIG.copy()
app_defaults['captain.age']  = 42

initialize_cliquet(config, __version__, defaults=app_defaults)
```

Use ``registry.setting('setting_name')``

```python
def read_setting(settings, defaults):
    def with_default(name):
         env_name = name.replace('.', '_').upper()
         setted = settings.get(name, defaults.get(name, ''))
         value = os.getenv(env_name, setted)
         if not value:
                  warnings.warn('Value not set for %s' % name)
    return lamdba name: with_default(name)

registry.setting = read_setting(settings)
```
